ctx: The essential polite greeting. Say this immediately upon entering any shop.
Use this instead of Bonjour starting around 5:00 PM.
Always append this to any request or order.
Standard polite gratitude.
The standard friendly response to 'Merci'.

en: Excuse me, I have a minor unexpected delay.
ctx: Polite phrase to warn a host or restaurant that you are running slightly late.
Always use this pronoun with shopkeepers, servers, and strangers.
Cultural norm: arrive 15 minutes late to dinner at private homes, never early.
Said when introduced to someone new.
Polite inquiry before switching languages.

fr: Une carafe d'eau, s'il vous plaît
en: A pitcher of tap water, please
ctx: How to get free water at restaurants. Avoid asking for 'un verre d'eau'.
Request this when ready to leave. Servers will not bring it automatically.

en: An espresso with a drop of milk
ctx: Like an Italian macchiato. Perfect mid-day coffee order.

Steak cooking temperatures.

Set lunchtime menu offering excellent value.
The chef's special dish for the day.
Ask to verify if a dish contains spicy seasoning.

fr: Je suis allergique aux fruits à coque.

en: That will be all, thank you.
ctx: Polite way to decline more food/drinks at the end of a meal.

fr: Un verre de vin rouge, s'il vous plaît.

Sells transit tickets, newspapers, and stamps.
False friend: this is a bookstore, not a library (bibliothèque).
Sells pens, paper, and office supplies.

Confirming payment method in smaller shops.

fr: Je voudrais un sac, s'il vous plaît.

Requesting a color treatment.

Requesting layered haircut styling.

Fuel rental policy; return with a fully filled tank.
Pumps marked Gazole are diesel. Do not use Sans Plomb.
Look for this when parked in a 'PAYANT' street.
Required for restricted free 'Zone Bleue' parking.
Pay on foot before returning to your car in garages.

fr: La voie avec le symbole de carte bancaire

Automated night-pumps require payment directly at the terminal.

Issued for parking violations or transit evasion.
General direction instruction.

Use with friends or young people only. Never use with shopkeepers or strangers.
Standard polite farewell — always say this when leaving a shop.
Friendly farewell when you expect to see someone again.
Said at the end of the evening when parting for the night.
Said when leaving a shop or ending a daytime interaction — very appreciated.
Universal agreement phrase. Far more common than 'oui' in conversation.
How to introduce yourself.
Formal. Use with strangers and shopkeepers. 'Ça va ?' is casual.
Standard polite response to 'Comment allez-vous ?'
Warm affirmation — more enthusiastic than 'oui'.
Reassuring phrase — use when someone thanks you or apologizes.
Essential — say this clearly and slowly so the person knows to rephrase.
Ask politely when you missed something. Add 'plus lentement' for more slowly.
Essential when someone speaks too fast. Most French people will oblige.
Sets expectations and often earns goodwill from locals.
Useful small talk opener — French people respond warmly to tourists making an effort.
A genuine compliment that locals appreciate hearing.
Always say this when clinking glasses — it's considered rude not to make eye contact.
Said before eating — always expected from the host or server.
Evening farewell — use after 6pm when leaving a shop or restaurant.
Ask this for addresses, names, or anything you need to remember precisely.

fr: Y a-t-il quelqu'un qui parle anglais ?

Say this to any passerby — French people are generally helpful to lost tourists.
Essential navigation phrase for any city.
Open-ended direction request — follow with your destination.
What locals will say pointing you in the right direction.
Directional response you'll hear frequently.

fr: C'est au coin de la rue
ctx: Very common response when asking for nearby locations.

fr: Pouvez-vous me montrer sur la carte ?

ctx: Show your phone map and ask locals to point — bypasses language barriers entirely.

en: Which way to the city center?
ctx: Useful in any French town — the centre-ville is always the main hub.
Common landmark reference in directions.
Directional preposition you'll hear when getting directions.
Ask for this at the guichet for train travel.
Specify this if you only need to go one way.
Ask at the station — platforms (quais) can change last minute.
Always ask before sitting in an unreserved seat on a train.
Verify before boarding — French trains have multiple splits at junctions.
Ask at the guichet or check the departure board (tableau des départs).
Ask a fellow passenger when unsure — especially on buses.
What you'll hear on the PA — retard means delay.
Say this at the guichet — staff can often rebook at no extra charge.
For buying or changing tickets in person at a station.

fr: Dites-moi quand descendre, s'il vous plaît
en: Please tell me when to get off
ctx: Ask the driver or a fellow passenger on an unfamiliar bus route.
Ask at your hotel reception — they can call one or point to the nearest rank.

fr: Conduisez-moi à cette adresse, s'il vous plaît
en: Take me to this address, please
ctx: Show your phone screen with the address — eliminates pronunciation issues.
Always confirm which airport (Charles de Gaulle or Orly) in Paris.

fr: Mettez le compteur, s'il vous plaît
ctx: Always ask this — prevents overcharging on unofficial fares.
Use when you want to be dropped before the destination.
Tipping taxi drivers is appreciated though not obligatory in France.
Don't hesitate to ask — French drivers can be assertive.
Airports have fixed fares — always confirm before getting in.
Have it open and ready at check-in and passport control.
Airports use this word for check-in — different from hotel check-in.
Ask at the gate or check the departures board.
Use when contacting your hotel or ground transport to warn of late arrival.

fr: Je ne trouve pas mes bagages
ctx: Say this at the baggage claim desk — they'll file a lost luggage report.
State this at customs — say it clearly and confidently.

fr: Y a-t-il une navette pour la ville ?
en: Is there a shuttle to the city?
ctx: Many French airports have shuttle buses cheaper than taxis.
Ask at airports, hotels, and cafés — French establishments increasingly offer it.
State this on arrival — have your booking confirmation ready on your phone.

en: Do you have a room available?
ctx: For walk-in bookings without a reservation.
Specify this — some double rooms in France have twin beds by default.
Always ask before committing to a room at a smaller hotel.
French hotel breakfasts can be expensive — worth knowing if it's included.

fr: À quelle heure faut-il libérer la chambre ?
ctx: Standard check-out in France is noon — ask if you need a late check-out.
Always acceptable to ask at smaller hotels before committing.
Report this to reception — essential in summer.
Many French hotels still use physical keys or key cards.
Many French buildings have very small elevators — ask if you have heavy luggage.

en: Can I leave my bags here?
ctx: Most hotels will store bags before check-in or after check-out.
The formal way to request your bill and check out at a hotel.

fr: Y a-t-il un coffre-fort dans la chambre ?
en: Is there a safe in the room?
ctx: Worth asking when carrying valuables or a lot of cash.
French hotels often provide just one towel per person — ask for more.

fr: Le mot de passe Wi-Fi, s'il vous plaît
ctx: Ask at check-in — French hotels don't always display it prominently.

fr: Une table pour deux, s'il vous plaît
ctx: Say this on arrival — French restaurants seat you, not open seating.
'La carte' is the à la carte menu. 'Le menu' is the fixed price set menu — know the difference.
Ask your server — they know the best dishes and specials of the day.
Point at the menu when ordering — very effective strategy.
Use to exclude an ingredient — 'sans oignons' (without onions) etc.
Specify 'plate' (still) or 'gazeuse' (sparkling) — sparkling is the default if unspecified.
What the server will ask when you order water. 'Plate' = still, 'gazeuse' = sparkling.
French chefs and servers genuinely appreciate hearing this — say it freely.
Ask before ordering — some French restaurants refuse separate bills.
Service is usually included (15%) in France — check before leaving an extra tip.
Polite way to flag an error without making a scene.

en: I'd like to make a reservation
ctx: Essential for popular Paris restaurants — call or book online in advance.
Terrace seating is very popular in France — ask on arrival.

fr: La carte des vins, s'il vous plaît
ctx: Separate from the food menu in most French restaurants.
Useful if a dish arrives lukewarm — a polite way to send it back briefly.
Useful at markets where negotiation is possible.

en: Do you have this in another size?
ctx: French sizing differs from US — always try before buying.

en: Do you have this in another color?
ctx: Ask to see the full range — items are often kept in the back.
Say this and staff will actively guide you to appropriate items.
For finding groceries — French supermarkets (Monoprix, Carrefour) are excellent.
French shops often close for lunch 12-2pm and early on Sundays.
Ask before making a special trip — hours vary widely.
Gift wrapping (emballage cadeau) is a French tradition and usually free.
Appropriate at markets and independent shops — not in chain stores.

en: How much do I owe you?
ctx: Direct and polite way to ask the total at markets or informal settings.
Distributeur automatique is the French term for ATM cash machine.
French small shops often struggle with large bills — have change when possible.

fr: Je crois qu'il y a une erreur
ctx: Polite way to flag a billing error without being accusatory.
What the terminal or cashier will tell you — have a backup card.
Shout this loudly in a genuine emergency — French people will respond.
Use with any service — police, hotel, restaurant — to convey urgency.
Basic illness statement — follow with symptoms for better help.
Simple and clear — use with pointing to indicate location of pain.
Point to the location as you say this — works across language barriers.
Important symptom to communicate at a pharmacy or clinic.
For serious emergencies — call 15 (SAMU) for medical emergencies first.

fr: Je suis allergique à la pénicilline
ctx: Critical medical information — keep this phrase memorized or written down.
Report to police (commissariat) and get a declaration for insurance.
In serious situations — the US Embassy in Paris is at 2 Avenue Gabriel.

fr: Je ne me sens pas bien
ctx: Gentler way to express illness — useful when symptoms are mild.

fr: Y a-t-il un médecin qui parle anglais ?
en: Is there a doctor who speaks English?
ctx: Ask at your hotel — they usually have a list of English-speaking doctors nearby.
State this clearly in a shop dispute — French consumer protection law is strong.
Use firmly if being harassed — French people respect direct assertiveness.
Many French museums offer excellent guided tours — often free with entry.

en: How much is the entrance fee?
ctx: Ask before queuing — many French monuments are free on the first Sunday of the month.
Many French shops and museums have reduced Sunday hours — always check.

en: Could you take a photo of us?
ctx: Ask a fellow visitor — French people are usually happy to help.
Worth asking — French cultural venues often offer reduced rates for students and under-26s.

fr: Ça vaut la peine de visiter ?
ctx: Ask locals for honest opinions — they'll steer you away from tourist traps.
Ask this at the tourist office or check the door — hours change seasonally.

fr: Je voudrais un plan de la ville
en: I'd like a map of the city
ctx: Free city maps available at any Office de Tourisme — indispensable for exploring on foot.
A simple compliment to the day — opens friendly conversations with locals.
Opener for any complaint or issue — calm and neutral.
Use for broken equipment, appliances, keys, etc.

en: I've been waiting a long time
ctx: Polite but effective at restaurants or service counters when ignored.

en: I'd like to speak to the manager
ctx: Use as a last resort — French customer service escalates quickly with this phrase.
Your right under French consumer law within 14 days for most purchases.
Firm and direct — use when a situation clearly falls below standard.
